title: Detecting Exciton Condensation through Charge Transport in Semiconductor Heterostructures
abstract: Direct evidence of exciton condensation in semiconductor heterostructures remains elusive. Here we propose charge transport of doped carriers as a probe of exciton condensation in transition-metal dichalcogenide heterostructures and identify distinct experimental signatures. Condensation suppresses the phase space for carrier scattering, leading to a reduction in resistivity, that provides a general diagnostic of exciton condensation. In heterostructures with a tunable solid-state Feshbach resonance, condensate-induced hybridization between doped carriers and trion bound states qualitatively modifies transport. In particular, near resonance, this hybridization yields a negative effective mass and a corresponding sign reversal of the Hall resistivity.

summary_segments.text: The work proposes using the charge transport of doped carriers as an indirect probe of exciton condensation in transition-metal dichalcogenide heterostructures, where direct evidence has remained difficult to obtain.
Condensation is argued to suppress the available phase space for carrier scattering, producing a drop in resistivity that serves as a general transport diagnostic of the condensate.

In a setting with a tunable solid-state Feshbach resonance, hybridization between doped carriers and trion bound states is predicted to qualitatively reshape transport, yielding a negative effective mass and a sign reversal of the Hall resistivity near resonance.

summary_segments.note: situates exciton condensation within the broader effort to detect equilibrium pairing in TMD bilayers
summary_segments.text: Exciton condensates in spatially separated electron and hole layers have long been pursued because the neutral bound pairs leave no direct charge-transport signature, motivating proposals that exploit how a condensate modifies the surrounding charge response.

significance_segments.text: By tying condensation to concrete transport observables—reduced resistivity and a Hall-resistivity sign reversal—the proposal offers experimentally accessible signatures that do not rely on directly detecting the neutral condensate.

significance_segments.note: connects to the tunability of TMD heterostructure platforms
significance_segments.text: Solid-state Feshbach resonances in TMD heterostructures provide an in-situ knob over carrier-trion coupling, making the predicted hybridization effects a candidate route to identify and characterize condensation in tunable devices.

title: Equilibrium spin currents in altermagnet junctions: Josephson-like and anomalous transport
abstract: Altermagnets (AMs) offer a compelling platform for exploring novel spin-dependent phenomena in materials with zero net macroscopic magnetization. In this work, we theoretically investigate the emergence of equilibrium spin currents (ESCs) in two-dimensional AM heterostructures using a tight-binding lattice model. We first study an AM-normal metal-AM (AM-NM-AM) junction and demonstrate that the sigma_y-polarized ESC exhibits a characteristic Josephson-like behavior, fundamentally governed by the relative angle between the Néel vectors of the two AMs. Replacing the central normal metal with a p-wave magnet (PM) induces an anomalous ESC; analogous to the anomalous Josephson effect, breaking of spatial inversion symmetry by the PM allows a finite, dissipationless spin current to flow even when the Néel vectors are perfectly aligned.

summary_segments.text: Using a tight-binding lattice model, the paper theoretically studies equilibrium spin currents in two-dimensional altermagnet heterostructures, a class of materials that combine spin-split bands with vanishing net magnetization.

For an altermagnet–normal-metal–altermagnet junction, the sigma_y-polarized equilibrium spin current shows a Josephson-like dependence controlled by the relative angle between the Néel vectors of the two altermagnets.

Replacing the central normal metal with a p-wave magnet produces an anomalous equilibrium spin current: in analogy with the anomalous Josephson effect, inversion-symmetry breaking by the p-wave magnet permits a dissipationless spin current even when the Néel vectors are collinear.

summary_segments.note: places altermagnets within recent spintronics interest
summary_segments.text: Altermagnets have attracted attention as a magnetic phase whose momentum-dependent spin splitting arises without net magnetization, opening routes to spin-dependent effects ordinarily associated with ferromagnets or noncollinear orders.

significance_segments.text: Identifying a Josephson-like spin-current relation set by the Néel-vector angle, and an anomalous spin current enabled by inversion breaking, provides concrete equilibrium transport signatures that could distinguish altermagnetic and p-wave-magnet junctions.
Equilibrium, dissipationless spin currents are of interest for low-power spintronic concepts, and junction geometries offer a controllable platform to test how emerging magnetic orders carry spin.

title: Simultaneous nanoscale imaging of local conductivity and chemical potential in a quantum Hall isospin ferromagnet
abstract: Quantum Hall isospin ferromagnetism in multilayer graphene offers a versatile playground for exploring flat band correlated physics, driven by the intricate coupling of spin, valley, orbital, and layer degrees of freedom. Here we introduce scanning conductivity and chemical potential microscopy (SCCM) and demonstrate it by probing quantum Hall states in bilayer graphene. We reveal a cascade of quantum Hall isospin ferromagnetic states with unexpected re-emergence behaviors in marginally twisted double bilayer graphene, establishing SCCM as a powerful spatially-resolved local probe of flat band correlated systems.

summary_segments.text: The authors introduce scanning conductivity and chemical potential microscopy (SCCM), a scanning-probe technique that simultaneously maps local conductivity and chemical potential, and benchmark it on quantum Hall states in bilayer graphene.

Applied to marginally twisted double bilayer graphene, SCCM resolves a cascade of quantum Hall isospin ferromagnetic states, including unexpected re-emergence of states as a function of the tuning parameters.
The results establish SCCM as a spatially resolved local probe well suited to flat-band correlated systems, where spin, valley, orbital, and layer degrees of freedom are intertwined.

summary_segments.note: explains why local probes matter for quantum Hall ferromagnetism
summary_segments.text: Quantum Hall isospin ferromagnetism in multilayer graphene involves competing symmetry-broken states whose spatial inhomogeneity is often averaged out in bulk transport, motivating local probes that combine conductivity and thermodynamic (chemical-potential) information.

significance_segments.text: By delivering simultaneous local conductivity and chemical-potential maps, SCCM adds a measurement modality for flat-band systems and reveals re-emergent isospin ferromagnetic states not obvious from global transport.

Marginally twisted multilayer graphene is a leading platform for correlated flat-band physics, and spatially resolved thermodynamic probes can help disentangle the closely spaced symmetry-broken phases that characterize these systems.

title: Anatomy of fast current-induced skyrmion motion in synthetic antiferromagnets
abstract: The high mobility of current-driven skyrmions in synthetic antiferromagnets (SAFs) is widely explained by the macroscopic suppression of the skyrmion Hall effect through gyrotropic force compensation. This established view, however, overlooks a concurrent and significant reduction in the Gilbert damping parameter, a key factor in the Thiele equation governing skyrmion velocity. Here we show that this damping attenuation originates from a reconfigured magnon-electron scattering landscape. Using a microscopic s-d model, we demonstrate that the strong antiferromagnetic interlayer RKKY exchange coupling in SAFs increases the magnonic gap of skyrmion collective modes, thereby suppressing the thermal magnon population and, consequently, the magnon-electron scattering rate. This provides a comprehensive microscopic understanding of fast skyrmion motion in SAFs.

summary_segments.text: The paper argues that the standard explanation for fast current-driven skyrmion motion in synthetic antiferromagnets—suppression of the skyrmion Hall effect via gyrotropic-force compensation—overlooks a simultaneous reduction in the Gilbert damping that also enters the Thiele equation for velocity.
Using a microscopic s-d model, the authors trace this damping reduction to a reconfigured magnon-electron scattering landscape rather than to the Hall-angle cancellation alone.

Strong antiferromagnetic interlayer RKKY coupling is shown to raise the magnonic gap of the skyrmion collective modes, depopulating thermal magnons and thereby lowering the magnon-electron scattering rate that controls damping.

summary_segments.note: why synthetic antiferromagnets are studied for racetrack-type devices
summary_segments.text: Synthetic antiferromagnets are pursued for spintronic memory because compensating the skyrmion Hall effect allows straight, fast current-driven motion; understanding the microscopic origin of their high mobility informs the design of such devices.

significance_segments.text: Attributing part of the enhanced skyrmion mobility to RKKY-induced suppression of Gilbert damping refines the standard picture and identifies interlayer exchange as a tunable lever on skyrmion dynamics.
A microscopic handle on what sets damping in synthetic antiferromagnets is relevant to engineering faster, more reliable skyrmion motion for proposed racetrack memory and logic concepts.

title: Ferromagnetism from the geometry of localized wavefunctions in moiré systems
abstract: We present a mechanism for ferromagnetism in narrow bands consisting of Anderson-localized states, exploiting single-particle localization to derive a controlled theory of exchange interactions within the narrow band. For quasiperiodic systems with a half-filled moiré band, the critical interaction strength for ferromagnetism is highly sensitive to the geometry of real-space overlaps between localized orbitals, with well-defined resonances at which ferromagnetism sets in for interaction energies far lower than the gap to other bands. Near these resonances, all approximations in the theory are controlled, making the critical point predictions quantitative.
arxiv_affiliations: Princeton Center for Theoretical Science, Princeton University
Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Princeton University

summary_segments.text: The authors propose a mechanism for ferromagnetism in narrow bands built from Anderson-localized states, using single-particle localization to construct a controlled theory of exchange interactions within the narrow band.

For quasiperiodic systems at half-filling of a moiré band, the critical interaction strength for ferromagnetism depends sensitively on the geometry of real-space overlaps between localized orbitals, with resonances where ferromagnetism onsets at interaction energies well below the gap to other bands.
Near these resonances the theory's approximations are controlled, allowing quantitative predictions for the critical point.

summary_segments.note: links wavefunction geometry to flat-band magnetism
summary_segments.text: Flat and narrow moiré bands host correlated magnetic phases whose stability is shaped by the spatial structure of the underlying wavefunctions, making the geometry of localized orbitals a central ingredient in theories of interaction-driven ordering.

significance_segments.text: By identifying resonances where wavefunction geometry drives ferromagnetism at unusually weak interactions, the work supplies a controlled, quantitative handle on flat-band magnetism rather than relying on phenomenological flat-band arguments alone.

significance_segments.note: connection to quasiperiodic and moiré platforms
significance_segments.text: Quasiperiodic moiré systems offer tunable localization, so a theory tying magnetic onset to real-space orbital overlaps suggests concrete knobs—geometry and filling—for engineering correlated magnetic states.

title: Interfacial Coupling and Sparse Intercalation of 7-Atom-Wide Armchair Graphene Nanoribbons by N-Heterocyclic Carbene Monolayers
abstract: Graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) synthesized on metal substrates experience electronic coupling and screening from the underlying surface, which can modify their observed properties and complicate their transfer to device-compatible substrates. Intercalation of GNRs by self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) offers a possible route to reduce this interaction. Here we investigate the intercalation of 7-atom-wide armchair graphene nanoribbons (7-AGNRs) on Au(111) using N-heterocyclic carbenes (NHCs), combining scanning tunneling microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and density functional theory calculations.
arxiv_affiliations: EMPA — Swiss Federal Laboratories for Materials Science and Technology

summary_segments.text: Graphene nanoribbons grown on metal substrates are electronically coupled to and screened by the surface, which alters their intrinsic properties and complicates transfer to device substrates; the authors pursue intercalation by self-assembled monolayers as a way to decouple them.

The study examines intercalation of 7-atom-wide armchair graphene nanoribbons on Au(111) by N-heterocyclic carbene monolayers, characterizing interfacial coupling and the sparse nature of the intercalation.
The analysis combines scanning tunneling microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and density functional theory to assess how the carbene layer modifies the ribbon-substrate interface.

summary_segments.note: explains the decoupling challenge for atomically precise GNRs
summary_segments.text: On-surface synthesis yields atomically precise armchair graphene nanoribbons whose electronic structure—including their width-dependent gap—is partially masked by hybridization with the underlying metal, so strategies to lift the ribbons off the substrate are a prerequisite for studying and using their intrinsic properties.

significance_segments.text: Demonstrating N-heterocyclic carbene monolayers as an intercalation layer for 7-AGNRs on Au(111) probes a chemically defined route toward reducing ribbon-substrate coupling, a step relevant to eventual device integration.

Decoupling and transferring atomically precise graphene nanoribbons is a long-standing bottleneck for realizing their predicted electronic behavior in devices, so interfacial chemistries that weaken substrate coupling are of broad interest for carbon-based nanoelectronics.

title: Hidden antiferromagnetism, persistent valley fluctuations, and U(6) crossovers in triangular-lattice M-point moiré materials via determinantal quantum Monte Carlo
abstract: A new moiré material platform has been recently proposed based on twisting two-dimensional atomic monolayers whose low-energy states lie at the three M-points of the Brillouin Zone. Continuum and ab initio modeling suggest that electrons in the conduction bands of these materials realize three-valley Hubbard models with valley-selective, quasi-one-dimensional hopping. Remarkably, the onsite Hubbard repulsion is almost U(6)-symmetric without fine-tuning. Here we show that this class of systems naturally admits sign-free determinantal quantum Monte Carlo simulations at a filling of three electrons per moiré unit cell, and explore the phase diagram for interactions of various strengths and U(6)-breaking anisotropies. We find a hidden antiferromagnetic order parameter and persistent valley fluctuations across the phase diagram.

summary_segments.text: The work studies a recently proposed moiré platform built from twisting monolayers whose band-edge states sit at the three M-points of the Brillouin zone, where the conduction-band electrons are modeled as a three-valley Hubbard system with valley-selective, quasi-one-dimensional hopping.

The authors note that the onsite repulsion in these models is nearly U(6)-symmetric without fine-tuning, and show that at a filling of three electrons per moiré cell the problem admits sign-free determinantal quantum Monte Carlo simulation.

Mapping the phase diagram across interaction strengths and U(6)-breaking anisotropies, they identify a hidden antiferromagnetic order parameter together with valley fluctuations that persist throughout the diagram.

summary_segments.note: explains why sign-free QMC access to a moiré model is notable
summary_segments.text: Most realistic moiré Hubbard models suffer from the fermion sign problem away from special fillings, so identifying a physically motivated platform that remains sign-free lets numerically exact methods, rather than approximate treatments, settle the nature of the correlated ground state.

significance_segments.text: Demonstrating sign-free DQMC access to a three-valley, near-U(6) moiré Hubbard model and uncovering hidden antiferromagnetism with persistent valley fluctuations provides numerically exact benchmarks for the correlated physics expected in M-point moiré materials.

Enlarged-symmetry Hubbard models are of broad interest as testing grounds for exotic ordering and competing fluctuations, and a controlled lattice realization with quantitative numerics offers a reference point for theories of multivalley moiré systems.

title: Hall conductivity reveals the nature of quantum coherence in strongly correlated metals
abstract: Linear-in-temperature resistivity is a hallmark for strange metallic transport, and appears universally in many strongly correlated electron systems. However, the focus on the longitudinal channel often overshadows the profound microscopic insights contained within the transverse response. Here, we utilize numerically exact determinantal quantum Monte Carlo simulations of the doped Hubbard model in a magnetic field to calculate longitudinal and transverse transport. While the resistivity is robustly T-linear across parameter sets, the Hall response is highly sensitive to particle-hole asymmetry, Fermi surface topology, and many-body correlation effects. Specifically, the combination of these effects determine a crossover scale in which the system becomes quantum-coherent, and is reflected in the Hall conductivity.
arxiv_affiliations: Stanford Institute for Materials and Energy Sciences, SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory
Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Stanford University

summary_segments.text: Motivated by the ubiquity of linear-in-temperature resistivity in strange metals, the authors argue that the transverse (Hall) channel carries microscopic information often missed by the usual focus on longitudinal transport.

They use numerically exact determinantal quantum Monte Carlo on the doped Hubbard model in a magnetic field to compute both longitudinal and Hall transport, finding the resistivity robustly T-linear across parameter sets while the Hall response is far more sensitive to particle-hole asymmetry, Fermi-surface topology, and many-body correlations.
The interplay of these effects sets a crossover scale at which the system becomes quantum-coherent, a scale that is imprinted on the Hall conductivity.

summary_segments.note: why the Hall channel is informative for correlated metals
summary_segments.text: The Hall response probes the curvature and sign structure of carrier dynamics rather than just scattering rates, so it can discriminate between microscopic scenarios that produce identical T-linear longitudinal resistivity, a long-standing ambiguity in strange-metal phenomenology.

significance_segments.text: By showing that the Hall conductivity, unlike the T-linear resistivity, encodes a coherence crossover sensitive to correlations and Fermi-surface topology, the work proposes the transverse channel as a sharper numerically grounded diagnostic of strange-metal physics.

Distinguishing among proposed origins of strange-metal transport is a central open question in correlated-electron physics, and exact simulations that tie an observable to the onset of quantum coherence give experiments a concrete target beyond the longitudinal resistivity.

title: Engineering electrically-switchable quantum anomalous Hall states by spin-orbit coupling
abstract: Nonvolatile gate-driven switching of quantum anomalous Hall (QAH) states in graphene moiré systems provides a promising route toward topological electronics based on chiral edge states. However, deliberate use of this switching mechanism requires control over both the magnetic properties and metastability of QAH states. While previous demonstrations mostly relied on the intrinsic magnetic energy landscape of moiré devices, this landscape can be engineered through proximity coupling to WSe2. Proximitizing twisted monolayer-bilayer graphene by WSe2 reshapes the magnetization reversals responsible for nonvolatile electrical switching of QAH states. This effect is attributed to the proximity-induced spin-orbit coupling (SOC), which can lock spin and valley and modify the magnetization of the competing states involved in switching compared with non-proximitized graphene systems.
arxiv_affiliations: Institute of Science and Technology Austria
Rudolf Peierls Centre for Theoretical Physics, University of Oxford
National Institute for Materials Science, Japan
groups: Hryhoriy Polshyn (Institute of Science and Technology Austria)

summary_segments.text: The paper addresses nonvolatile, gate-driven switching of quantum anomalous Hall states in graphene moiré systems, noting that exploiting this switching deliberately requires control over both the magnetic properties and the metastability of the QAH states.

Rather than relying on the device's intrinsic magnetic energy landscape, the authors engineer that landscape by proximity-coupling twisted monolayer-bilayer graphene to WSe2, which reshapes the magnetization reversals that underlie nonvolatile electrical switching.

They attribute the effect to proximity-induced spin-orbit coupling, which can lock spin and valley and alter the magnetization of the competing states involved in switching relative to non-proximitized graphene.

summary_segments.note: links proximity SOC to topological switching
summary_segments.text: Placing a transition-metal dichalcogenide adjacent to graphene imprints spin-orbit coupling without disrupting the moiré flat bands, a now-common strategy for tuning the magnetic and topological character of correlated graphene devices.

significance_segments.text: Showing that proximity-induced spin-orbit coupling reshapes the magnetization reversals governing QAH switching turns an intrinsic device property into an engineerable knob, advancing controllable nonvolatile topological switching based on chiral edge states.

Electrically switchable, nonvolatile quantum anomalous Hall states are an attractive primitive for low-dissipation topological electronics, so demonstrating a materials route to control their magnetic metastability is a step toward usable devices.

title: Revealing quantum geometry effects in magic angle twisted bilayer graphene using the circular photogalvanic effect
abstract: We report photocurrent studies of a magic angle twisted bilayer graphene device using near infrared light. Through photocurrent imaging and polarization dependence, we separate the photo-thermoelectric effect from the photogalvanic effect. We observe a circular photogalvanic effect (CPGE) over a wide range of doping and temperature. The CPGE at normal incidence constrains the symmetry of the system to C1, and points to a Berry curvature dipole, in agreement with theoretical predictions for strained graphene. Remarkably, the CPGE vanishes for filling -2.5 < nu < -1.5, suggesting an additional symmetry breaking in that regime.

summary_segments.text: The authors perform photocurrent measurements on a magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene device using near-infrared light, and through photocurrent imaging and polarization dependence separate the photo-thermoelectric contribution from the photogalvanic one.

They observe a circular photogalvanic effect across a broad range of doping and temperature; its presence at normal incidence constrains the device symmetry to C1 and indicates a Berry curvature dipole consistent with theoretical expectations for strained graphene.
Notably, the circular photogalvanic effect disappears in a window of filling near three-quarters of a hole away from charge neutrality, pointing to an additional symmetry breaking in that regime.

summary_segments.note: explains why CPGE probes quantum geometry
summary_segments.text: The circular photogalvanic effect is a symmetry-sensitive nonlinear response whose normal-incidence component can reflect a Berry curvature dipole, making polarization-resolved photocurrent a window onto the quantum geometry and symmetry of flat-band moiré systems.

significance_segments.text: Detecting a doping-dependent circular photogalvanic effect that constrains the symmetry to C1 and signals a Berry curvature dipole gives an optical, symmetry-resolved probe of quantum geometry in magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene, including a filling window where the response vanishes.

Quantum geometric quantities such as Berry curvature shape the correlated and topological phases of magic-angle graphene, so optical probes that are directly sensitive to these quantities complement transport in mapping symmetry breaking across the phase diagram.

title: 1/3 Fractional and Gapless Integer Quantum Anomalous Hall States in Rhombohedral Graphene
abstract: The fractional quantum anomalous Hall (FQAH) effect occurs in moiré superlattices in both twisted bilayer MoTe2 and rhombohedral n-layer graphene aligned to hexagonal boron nitride (RnG/hBN) as a novel quantum phase driven by intertwined electron correlation and topology. Although several fractional states in the Jain sequence have been identified, the 1/3 state, the most robust and fundamental state in conventional fractional quantum Hall (FQH) systems, was missing in either FQAH system. Here we report the FQAH effect at moiré filling factor nu=1/3 in R5G/hBN moiré superlattice devices, through a combination of quantum capacitance and transport measurements. The FQAH states in R5G/hBN now exhibit a surprising level of particle-hole symmetry about half-filling, closely resembling the behavior of FQH states in the lowest Landau level.
groups: Raymond C. Ashoori (Massachusetts Institute of Technology)
